critic_raw:
The RNN versus logistic regression equipment failure prediction design has a label construction problem that creates target leakage risk. The positive label is assigned to any sensor sequence occurring within 168 hours (7 days) before a logged failure. The negative label is all other sequences. However, sensor sequences are time series windows, and if the feature engineering computes rolling statistics over 1-hour and 24-hour windows using data that extends close to the failure event, there is implicit temporal overlap between the feature window and the label window for sequences at the boundary of the 7-day pre-failure period. The design does not specify whether the feature computation window and the label window are checked for independence, and this boundary condition creates a potential for near-future failure information to contaminate features through the rolling statistics.

The RNN uses a 168-hour lookback window while the logistic regression uses the same 9 engineered features averaged or summarized over the same 168-hour window. This means the logistic regression discards all temporal structure in the window and works only on aggregate statistics. A stronger comparison would use gradient boosted trees or a time series feature set (e.g., tsfresh-generated features) on the same window, since these approaches can capture distributional shape without the full sequential architecture. The logistic regression baseline tests whether temporal feature summarization is sufficient, not whether temporal structure is necessary — the latter being the more scientifically interesting question.

With a 36-month dataset and a 7-day pre-failure label window, the proportion of positive labels depends entirely on the failure frequency of the machines. The design does not report expected failure rates, making it impossible to assess class imbalance severity. Industrial equipment failure prediction datasets routinely have <1% positive rate, which would make F1 score highly sensitive to threshold selection and potentially dominated by precision-recall trade-off artifacts rather than predictive quality.

The test set covers only 6 months (months 31–36) of data. For machines with infrequent failures, the 6-month test window may contain only a handful of failure events per machine type. With stratification by machine type, the per-machine-type F1 scores will be computed on very small positive samples, making cross-machine-type comparison unreliable and the 95% bootstrap confidence intervals extremely wide.
defender_raw:
The RNN design for industrial equipment failure prediction is well-matched to the temporal structure of sensor data in SCADA systems. The 7-day pre-failure labeling window is not arbitrary — it is explicitly justified by the plant's maintenance scheduling cycle, ensuring that the prediction horizon is operationally useful. A shorter window would produce labels too close to failure for preventive action; a longer window would capture too much normal operation within the positive class. The alignment between the prediction window and business process constraints is a design strength that is often overlooked by reviewers focused on algorithmic details.

The feature engineering is disciplined and appropriate: rolling statistics at both 1-hour and 24-hour windows capture both short-term anomaly signatures and longer-term drift trends, while first-order differences capture rate-of-change dynamics that static snapshots miss. Using the same engineered features as inputs to logistic regression (flattened) ensures the feature quality is held constant between models — the only experimental variable is the sequential modeling capacity of the RNN. This design directly addresses the criticism that neural models often win because of better features rather than better architectures.

Stratifying the temporal split by machine type is a critical design choice for a multi-equipment facility. Different machine types have heterogeneous failure modes, sensor distributions, and failure frequencies. Without this stratification, rare machine types could be absent from validation or test sets, producing artificially easy or artificially hard evaluations for specific equipment. The per-machine-type F1 as a secondary metric allows the operator to see exactly which machines benefit from sequential modeling — operationally specific information that a single aggregate metric would obscure.

F1 as the primary metric balances precision (avoiding unnecessary maintenance dispatch) and recall (catching genuine failures), directly reflecting the dual-cost structure of industrial predictive maintenance. Matthews Correlation Coefficient as an additional secondary metric provides robustness to class imbalance that F1 alone can miss under extreme failure rarity.
all_issues_raised: Feature rolling windows computed near the boundary of the 7-day failure label window may include implicit temporal overlap, creating near-future leakage
Logistic regression baseline on aggregated statistics is too weak; does not test the hypothesis that temporal structure per se is necessary vs. richer feature engineering
Expected positive class rate (failure frequency) not reported; class imbalance severity could be extreme (<1%), making F1 primary metric highly threshold-sensitive
6-month test window may contain only a few failure events per machine type, making per-machine-type stratified F1 unreliable
No specified mechanism for handling sensor recalibration events that could generate transient anomalies falsely resembling pre-failure signatures
all_issues_adjudicated: Feature rolling windows computed near the boundary of the 7-day failure label window may include implicit temporal overlap, creating near-future leakage
Expected positive class rate (failure frequency) not reported; class imbalance severity could be extreme (<1%), making F1 primary metric highly threshold-sensitive
6-month test window may contain only a few failure events per machine type, making per-machine-type stratified F1 unreliable
